message.content:
{"html":"

Marking Feedback

Total Score: 68 / 100
This shows good understanding overall, but errors in language make it developing in places. Real exam standard: borderline Good.

Category Scores

CategoryScore/ MaxLevel (Error Rate)
Spelling & Punctuation2430Good (6%)
Grammar2635Good (7%)
Phrasing1835Developing (11%)

What You Did Well

  • Clear structure with 'First of all', 'Secondly' etc. - helps the reader follow your points.
  • Good choice of quotes like 'snuggled' and 'pure white goose' to back up ideas.
  • Your ideas about Willy (colour, troublemaker, protector, beauty) show you understand the text.

Specific Improvements

  • Fix spelling and add spaces after commas/periods to make it look neater.
  • Match verbs to subjects (e.g. he 'bring' should fit singular).
  • Shorten long sentences and make phrasing smoother for better flow.

Observations on Clarity & Style

  • Ideas are clear but long sentences make some parts hard to read quickly.
  • Some phrasing feels clumsy, like jumbled ideas in one chunk.
  • Linking words are used well, but tense jumps confuse the timeline.
  • Overall, meaning comes through, but polishing would boost scores.
  • Style is enthusiastic - keep that energy!

Patterns of Errors & Actionable Tips

  • Subject-verb agreement: Make sure verb matches subject. E.g. 'writer present' → 'writer presents'.
  • Spelling patterns: Double-check animal words and adverbs. Practice common ones.
  • Run-on sentences: Split long ones. E.g. 'Since...upside down. This...' → two shorter sentences.
  • Missing words/articles: Add 'a/an/the'. E.g. 'unexpected way' → 'an unexpected way'.

All Spelling Mistakes (Check These in a Dictionary)

  • ducklin
  • hads
  • attened
  • sggests
  • Thridly
  • sheild
  • uncompared
  • paralell
  • memoried

Keep Going! You're on the right track with text analysis - just tidy up language and you'll smash higher bands. Practice one category at a time!

The task is to mark the student's answer based on the provided mark scheme for language proficiency: Spelling & Punctuation (30 points), Grammar (35 points), Phrasing (35 points). Total maximum is 100...Since ExistingSummary is empty, you can create any subheading title you like, as long as it follows the guidelines.

## Reviewing task details
- The task involves marking a student's answer based on a language proficiency scheme: Spelling & Punctuation (30 points), Grammar (35 points), Phrasing (35 points), with a total of 100 points.
- The prompt mentions a "Maximum Mark: 3.0," which may be an error, so the focus is on the 100-point scheme from the model answer.
- The output must be in valid JSON with specific keys: "html," "feedback," "links," "score," and "score_comment."

## Preparing feedback format
- The "html" field will contain a beautifully formatted feedback fragment, including headings and category scores.
- Feedback will be a list of items, each with an "id," "text," "type," and "severity," linked to "chunk_ids."
- The score can be a number or null, with an optional "score_comment" for additional context.

## Analyzing student answer
- First, concatenate all chunks to form the full text for evaluation.
- The initial text provided is: "First of all, the writer present his idea that Willy bring colour to his boring life from the moment he was born. He used 'snuggled' and 'keep it at eye level' suggest that they both give." 
- Further analysis is needed to assess Spelling & Punctuation, Grammar, and Phrasing based on the scheme.<|eos|>If you need to create a new subheading and cannot think of a unique title, use "Investigating" as a fallback, but try to be more specific first.

---

## Identifying spelling errors
- The text contains spelling errors like "ducklin" (should be "duckling"), "hads" (should be "has"), and "attened" (should be "attended").
- Other mistakes include "sggests" (should be "suggests"), "Thridly" (should be "Thirdly"), and "sheild" (should be "shield").

## Checking grammar issues
- Grammar errors include "tell" (should be "tells") and potential verb form issues like "serve" (may need "served").
- Inconsistent capitalization, such as "WIlly" with a capital "I," may also affect grammar scoring.

## Evaluating phrasing and context
- Phrases like "uncompared" may be non-standard, possibly meaning "unparalleled," and "unexceptionally" could be a misspelling of "exceptionally."
- Context suggests some words, like "memoried," are unclear or incorrect, impacting phrasing assessment.<|eos|>If you cannot think of a unique title, use "Investigating" as a fallback, but try to be more specific first.

- "WIlly" appears multiple times (e.g., chunks 8, 11) with inconsistent capitalization, likely a spelling error for "Willy."
- Additional errors include "paralell" (should be "parallel") and "memoried" (likely "memories"), affecting the spelling score.

## Checking punctuation issues
- Many punctuation errors, such as missing spaces after commas (e.g., "him,shifting") and run-on sentences, impact clarity.
- Periods and commas are often misplaced, like "upside down.This," reducing overall punctuation marks.

## Assessing grammar problems
- Subject-verb agreement issues include "hads" (should be "has"), "tell" (should be "tells"), and "bring" (should be "brings").
- Other grammar errors involve incorrect verb forms, like "can compared" (should be "can be compared"), affecting the grammar score.<|eos|>If you cannot think of a unique title, use "Investigating" as a fallback, but try to be more specific first.

## Assessing grammar problems
- Tense errors are frequent, such as "was reliable protector" (should be "was a reliable protector") and "his whole life to the author" (likely "devoted his whole life to the author").
- Articles are often missing, like in "memoried" (should be "memories"), impacting grammar assessment.
